summary: Alveolar bone loss is primarily caused by periodontal disease (chronic bacterial infection). Treatment ranges from non-surgical scaling and root planing (SRP) for mild-moderate cases to surgical intervention for advanced disease. SRP achieves 1-2mm attachment gain in moderate pockets. Maintenance therapy every 3-4 months is critical for long-term stability. Bone grafting may be indicated for significant vertical defects.
risk_factors: Poor oral hygiene and irregular maintenance visits
Tooth mobility grade II or higher
alternatives: Guided tissue regeneration (GTR) with membrane and bone graft
Enamel matrix derivative (Emdogain) for intrabony defects
Systemic or local antibiotic adjunct therapy (minocycline microspheres)
referral_summary: Refer to periodontist for: pockets >6mm not responding to SRP, vertical bone defects requiring regenerative surgery, furcation involvement grade II-III, or patients with systemic conditions complicating periodontal management.
patient_education: Bone loss around your teeth is caused by gum disease — a bacterial infection that gradually destroys the bone supporting your teeth. Treatment starts with deep cleaning below the gumline to remove bacteria and tartar. You'll need to maintain excellent home care: brush twice daily, floss every day, and use any prescribed mouth rinse. Regular professional cleanings every 3-4 months are essential to prevent further bone loss. If caught early, the bone can stabilize; in advanced cases, surgery may help regenerate lost bone.
